quotes.text: Algonquian peoples warned of the wendigo — a starving spirit that drove travelers to madness in the deep snow.
European folklore held that iron, salt, and church bells could ward off the fae — every household kept some by the door.
Medieval bestiaries described the Questing Beast: the body of a leopard, the haunches of a lion, the feet of a hart, and a voice like thirty hounds.
Baba Yaga's hut walked on chicken legs and turned to face whoever spoke the right words.
Yorkshire ballads claim the Dragon of Wantley was killed by a knight in spiked armor — kicked once in the only soft spot.
Across Europe the Wild Hunt rode through winter skies — Odin, King Arthur, or the Devil, depending on whose grandmother you asked.
Indigenous nations of the Pacific Northwest carved the Thunderbird on poles — wings wide enough to darken the sky.
Scottish coastal folk swore that selkies shed their seal skins on the shore and danced as women under the moon.
